4fd3befb652205c6f51b5c0d23a240174459536f:
Gauntlet AI offers a fellowship program designed to help participants design and ship production-grade AI systems.  
The fellowship lasts for 10 weeks, consisting of 3 weeks remote and 7 weeks in-person in Austin, Texas.  
Participants receive weekly assignments on Monday and must ship their work by Friday, with no extensions allowed.  
The curriculum is divided into two phases: Phase 1 is remote and covers AI coding methodologies, legacy systems, and open-source AI agents.  
Phase 2 is on-site and includes projects for hiring partners, enterprise systems, legacy modernization, multimodal AI, and capstone projects.  
Travel, housing, food, compute, and model access are covered by hiring partners, and no tuition is charged to participants.  
Graduates of the program secure positions with starting compensation ranging from $200k to $950k.  
Eligibility for Gauntlet Prime requires 3+ years of professional software engineering experience and U.S. work authorization.  
Eligibility for Gauntlet for America requires 1+ year of professional software engineering experience and U.S. citizenship.  
The selection philosophy emphasizes judging candidates based on their output rather than their educational background.  
Cohort 5 starts on April 27, 2026, Cohort 6 starts on July 6, 2026, and Cohort 7 starts on September 14, 2026.  
Applications for the fellowship are reviewed on a rolling basis.  
Matthew Daw is a current participant in Cohort V (Cohort 5).

f46393bb5bf6373898f76f359ca121fa00fcdf08:
Matthew Daw holds a BS in Applied & Computational Mathematics Emphasis (ACME) from Brigham Young University, completed between 2019 and 2022.  
He was on the Dean's list, ranking in the top 5% of his class, and received a full-tuition scholarship.  
ACME is a rigorous, proof-based applied and computational mathematics major.  
The ACME curriculum is divided into four volumes, each covering distinct areas of mathematics and applications.  

ACME Volume 1 focuses on Mathematical Analysis, including Linear Analysis and Nonlinear Analysis.  
Linear Analysis I covers topics such as vector spaces, linear transformations, inner product spaces, and spectral theory.  
Nonlinear Analysis I includes metric space topology, differentiation, and contraction mappings.  
Nonlinear Analysis II covers multivariable integration, calculus on manifolds, and complex analysis.  
Linear Analysis II includes spectral calculus, iterative methods, and rings and polynomials.  

ACME Volume 2 is centered on Algorithms, Approximation, and Optimization.  
It introduces algorithms and analysis, including complexity, data structures, and combinatorial optimization.  
Approximation topics include harmonic analysis and polynomial approximation.  
Optimization covers unconstrained, linear, and nonlinear constrained optimization, as well as dynamic optimization.  

ACME Volume 3 addresses Probability, Statistics, and Machine Learning.  
It includes probabilistic models, classical inference, regression, and adaptive methods.  
Machine learning topics cover unsupervised methods, linear models, decision trees, and neural networks.  
Deep learning includes convolutional neural networks and reinforcement learning techniques.  

ACME Volume 4 focuses on Dynamic Modeling, PDEs, Calculus of Variations, and Optimal Control.  
Dynamic Modeling I covers mathematical modeling, existence and uniqueness of solutions, and stability theory.  
Partial Differential Equations include hyperbolic, parabolic, and elliptic equations, along with eigenfunction expansions.  
Calculus of Variations introduces optimization principles and conditions for extrema.  
Optimal Control covers Pontryagin's Maximum Principle and Linear Quadratic Regulators.  

Matthew's senior capstone project involved building a real-time learning algorithm to maximize language learning using Hidden Markov chains.  
He is also pursuing an incomplete MS in Computer Science at BYU, focusing on graph theory and deep learning.  
Additionally, he holds an Associate's degree in General Studies from Utah Valley University.
